mnemonics.title: Remember Relation Components
mnemonics.content: T A D - Tuples, Attributes, Domains. Think: 'TAD is the basic building block of a relation'.

mnemonics.title: Key Types Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: Super Key > Candidate Key > Primary Key. Remember: 'Super Candidates make Prime selections'.

mnemonics.title: Integrity Constraints
mnemonics.content: E.R.D. - Entity, Referential, Domain. Remember: 'Every Relational Database needs ERD for integrity'.

mnemonics.title: Relational Algebra Operations
mnemonics.content: S.P.U.D.R - Selection, Projection, Union, Difference (Set), Cartesian Product, Rename. Remember: 'Select, Project, Union, Differentiate, times, Rename'.

mnemonics.title: Relation Properties
mnemonics.content: C O U R T - Composite (no duplicate tuples), Ordered (no), Unique attributes, Rectangular (all cells), Tuples have unique identity. Remember: 'Data in a relation is COURTed to be proper'.

acronyms.usage: Standard language used to interact with relational databases based on relational algebra concepts.
Selected candidate key that uniquely identifies each tuple in a relation.
Attribute that references primary key of another relation to establish relationships.
Minimal super key that can become primary key; all candidate keys are super keys.
Represents missing or unknown values in the database.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a spreadsheet with column headers (attributes) and rows (tuples). Each cell intersection is an atomic value.
Draw an inverted triangle: Top = Super Key (largest), Middle = Candidate Key, Bottom = Primary Key (smallest, selected).
Draw two tables with an arrow from FK in child table pointing to PK in parent table, showing referential link.
Selection = horizontal slice (choosing rows), Projection = vertical slice (choosing columns). Think of a matrix being cut.
Entity Integrity = PK box in one table must be filled. Referential Integrity = FK box must match PK or be empty.

analogies.analogy: Think of a relation like a class attendance register - each row is a student (tuple), each column is an attribute like name, roll number, attendance.
Like Aadhaar/Social Security number - uniquely identifies one person among billions, never changes, never blank.
Like a library card number in a book borrowing record - it references the member's ID from the members table.
Like a dropdown menu in a form - only certain predefined values are allowed for that field.
Like a blank space on a form where the question doesn't apply or the answer is not known - it's neither '0' nor 'N/A' text.
